perturbation.original_text: a) that it shall treat the Confidential Information directly or indirectly disclosed to it by the Disclosing Party as strictly confidential and will not without obtaining the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party disclose any part of the Confidential Information to any third party, except where compelled by law or regulation
perturbation.changed_text: a) that it shall endeavor to treat the Confidential Information directly or indirectly disclosed to it by the Disclosing Party as confidential and will not, to a reasonable extent, disclose any part of the Confidential Information to any third party, except where compelled by law or regulation.
perturbation.explanation: The change from "shall treat ... as strictly confidential and will not" to "shall endeavor to treat ... as confidential and will not, to a reasonable extent" introduces ambiguity. "Endeavor" and "to a reasonable extent" are subjective and weaken the obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text weakens the obligation to keep information confidential, which can conflict with the Norwegian Trade Secrets Act § 4, requiring adequate measures to protect trade secrets, undermining the effectiveness of legal protection.

perturbation.justification: The original text mandates strict confidentiality, whereas the changed text introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation using terms like 'endeavor' and 'to a reasonable extent.' The LLM-generated law explanation suggests this weakening may not provide adequate measures to protect trade secrets, which conflicts with the Norwegian Trade Secrets Act § 4. Although no Norwegian Trade Secrets Act § 4 text is available from the scraped snippets, the change weakens a legal obligation.

perturbation.original_text: d) that it shall not copy or reproduce in any form any of the Confidential Information disclosed to it by the Disclosing Party, except to the extent necessary for the LOA purposes
perturbation.changed_text: d) that it will try to not copy or reproduce in any form any of the Confidential Information disclosed to it by the Disclosing Party, except where it is reasonably necessary for the LOA purposes.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall not copy' to 'will try to not copy' and 'to the extent necessary' to 'where it is reasonably necessary' weakens the obligation and introduces subjective interpretation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Copyright Act aims to protect the integrity of copyrighted material. Using the term 'try' and 'reasonably necessary' weakens the original clause which could be interpreted to allow for unauthorized use that would otherwise be illegal, thereby undermining the protection of copyrighted materials and directly contradicting Norwegian copyright laws.

perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity by changing 'shall not copy' to 'will try to not copy' and 'to the extent necessary' to 'where it is reasonably necessary'. This weakens the original obligation. The Law Explanation states that this weakening could be interpreted to allow for unauthorized use that would otherwise be illegal, thereby undermining the protection of copyrighted materials. Scraped Law Snippet 1, from Lovdata, refers to åndsverkloven (Copyright Act), and mentions 'Kapittel 1 Grunnleggende bestemmelser om opphavsrett til åndsverk', indicating that the law protects copyrighted works. The modification weakens this protection by introducing subjective interpretations of the obligation, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Clause 5 Either Party shall, upon 30 days written request by the other Party, return to the other party all Confidential Information which the latter has disclosed to the former, together with all copies or reproductions thereof together with a confirmation in writing that the Confidential Information in computerized form has been destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: Clause 5 Either Party should, where practically feasible upon request by the other Party, endeavor to return to the other party all Confidential Information which the latter has disclosed to the former, along with copies or reproductions to the best of their ability and confirmation that computerized Confidential Information has been addressed appropriately.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "shall" with "should, where practically feasible," "return" with "endeavor to return," "all copies" with "to the best of their ability," and "destroyed" with "addressed appropriately" introduce significant ambiguities. The requirement for destruction is now discretionary and open to interpretation.
perturbation.law_explanation: This directly contradicts the requirements stipulated by the Norwegian Personal Data Act § 13 which mandates the secure and complete destruction of personal data when it is no longer required for its initial purpose. The original clause ensured a standard of certainty which aligns with this law. The modified clause introduces ambiguity in handling data that could be considered personal information, making it harder to verify destruction and maintain privacy standards.

perturbation.justification: The original text mandated specific actions regarding confidential information, using 'shall' and 'destroyed,' which created a clear obligation. The changed text introduces ambiguities using 'should, where practically feasible,' 'endeavor to return,' 'to the best of their ability,' and 'addressed appropriately.' These changes weaken the obligation and introduce discretion. The Law Explanation argues that this contradicts the Norwegian Personal Data Act § 13, which mandates the secure and complete destruction of personal data. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to Personopplysningsloven, dealing with handling personal data, suggesting the changes could affect data privacy compliance. The modified contract makes compliance and enforcement less certain.

perturbation.original_text: Clause 8 The Receiving Party shall notify the Disclosing Party immediately if it becomes aware that Confidential Information has been disclosed to or is in the possession of any person who is not authorized by the NDA to receive Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Clause 8 The Receiving Party should consider notifying the Disclosing Party if it suspects that Confidential Information may have been disclosed to someone possibly not authorized by the NDA.
perturbation.explanation: The change from "shall notify immediately" to "should consider notifying if it suspects" weakens the obligation to report data breaches or unauthorized access.
perturbation.law_explanation: This alteration reduces the mandatory nature of reporting security breaches, which conflicts with the Norwegian Security Act § 5-1 that requires immediate reporting of any events that could compromise national security interests or classified information. By changing the language from a mandatory obligation to a discretionary action, this change undermines the effectiveness of security protocols.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: ut a time stamp from P.A.C.E.R., a federal courts database; Stone has suggested the lack of a time stamp shows CNN had advance knowledge of his arrest, and the filing reads: “a person with privileged access to a ‘draft’ of Roger Stone’s Indictment, identical to that which had been filed under seal…had — in violation of the Court’s Order — publicly distributed the Indictment prior to its release from the sealing ordered by the Court,” Tal Axelrod reports at the Hill . Two teams of federal officials convened to combat foreign election interference are being dramatically downsized, according to three current and former Department of Homeland Security officials. The sources claim that they fear the department will not prepare adequately for election threats in 2020, with one stating: “the clear assessment from the intelligence community is that 2020 is going to be the perfect storm … we know Russia is going to be engaged … so it’s very curious why the task forces were demoted in the bureaucracy and the leadership has not committed resources to prepare for the 2020 election,” Erin Banco and Betsy Woodruff report at The Daily Beast . GOVERNMENT SHUTDOWN AND BORDER WALL President Trump yesterday would not commit on whether he will sign a bipartisan border security deal reached this week but floated the possibility that he would move to unilaterally secure funding for his long-promised border wall, telling reporters that “regardless of what I do … I have a lot of options.” The president reiterated that he wants to avoid another government shutdown, saying that “I don’t want to see a shutdown …a shutdown would be a terrible thing,” and later adding that “there’s no reason” for a second shutdown within two months, Caitlin Oprysko reports at POLTICO . Lawmakers worked toward finalizing a significant spending bill and border security compromise yesterday, preparing to get it passed and sent to the president in time to avoid the possible shutdown Friday. Lawmakers finalized the 1,159-page bill just before midnight yesterday, with votes expected in the House and the Senate today; although the outcome remains uncertain, the legislation is expected to pass and Trump is expected to sign it, Erica Werner, John Wagner and Mike DeBonis report at the Washington Post . IRAN Former U.S. air force intelligence officer Monica Witt has been charged with espionage in addition to giving away the identity of a U.S. agent and other secrets. Witt (39,) was a cryptologist and a counter-intelligence investigator for the U.S. air force for over 10 years before working as an intelligence analyst for the defense contractor Booz Allen Hamilton; according to charges unsealed yesterday by the Department of Justice (D.O.J.,) she defected to Iran in August 2013, taking along details of U.S. counter-intelligence agents she had worked alongside who were then targeted by Iranian hackers, four of whom are named in the indictment and charged alongside Witt, Julian Borger reports at the Guardian. Witt allegedly conspired with Iranian associates to provide “documents and information relating to the national defense of the United States … with the intent and reason to believe that the same would be used to the injury of the U.S. and to the advantage of Iran,” in the period from around January 2012 to around May 2015, the indictment reads. Witt also “created target packages for use by Iran” against U.S. government agents and counterintelligence officers, the indictment alleges, Matt Zapotosky reports at the Washington Post . “It is a sad day for America when one of its citizens betrays our country,” Assistant Attorney General John Demers said yesterday, adding “it is sadder still when this person, as a member of the American armed forces, previously invoked the aid of God to bear true faith and allegiance to the Constitution of the U.S. and to defend her country against foreign enemies … Monica Witt is alleged to have done just this,” Nicole Gaouette and Tammy Kupperman report
perturbation.justification: The original text mandated immediate notification of unauthorized disclosure, while the changed text weakens this to a suggestion based on suspicion. The law explanation highlights that this undermines security protocols, referencing the Norwegian Security Act § 5-1, even though there isn't a corresponding scraped law snippet. The change introduces ambiguity and reduces the enforceability of the obligation to report breaches.
